sections.text: Mugasha 1 begins with seventeen lines of poetic images that are associated with the fisher-god or that foreshadow the events of the ballad (lines 1-3.2).
Mugasha speaks from inside his mother's womb demanding birth but refusing to be born; his difficult birth torments his mother (lines 4-5.3).
Midwives try various remedies, but none help (lines 6-18.1).
Finally, a midwife suggests consulting and sacrificing to Wamara, the king of gods, and this is done (lines 19-25.1).
Wamara instructs the petitioners [through a spirit medium] to build Mugasha a spirit shrine (lines 26-30.1).
Mugasha is born; he immediately sends his uncle on a journey to get him a canoe (lines 31-37.1).

The infant walks out to receive people and announces his intention to travel; people are amazed (lines 38-39.4). Mugasha goes to Lake Victoria, where he summons the nature spirits he controls and announces his intentions to fish (lines 40-7).
He sets out, but his mother prays to the Sun for his return, and he returns to shore (lines 48-50).
Mugasha lives on the lake for eight years and in the ninth decides to visit his mother (lines 51-54.1).
On the shore he sees nine women, but cannot tell which is his mother; he feigns drowning so his mother will reveal herself by saving him (lines 55-59.2).

[Missing in this version (because the singer has collapsed similar episodes) is the following action: Mugasha's mother's sister runs into the lake to save him but fearfully backs out when the water reaches her chest, leaving Mugasha still ignorant of his mother's identity. Then the god Wamara, who happens to be there, devises another test. To whoever will take his grandson Kagoro's copper spear and kill Mugasha, Wamara will grant nine children.]
Mugasha's mother's sister reasons that nine children outweigh one, takes the spear, but is prevented from throwing it by Nyambubi, Mugasha's mother. Nyambubi wins the trial (lines 60-6.3).
Mugasha returns to the lake and one day sees Wamara's daughter gathering straw. He proposes to her but she rejects him. He swears he will marry her; she swears he will not (lines 67-86.2).
Mugasha summons his minions, and they attend, identify themselves with praise epithets, and promise to assist Mugasha in his marriage plan (lines 87-102.1).
Mugasha asks them about the items of brideprice and other gifts needed for a wedding, and he makes substitutions for them with lake animals (lines 103-106.3).
At Wamara's palace, a party of lake dwellers arrives to arrange a marriage. Wamara rejects the suit, and with his power alters the appearance of several of the animals who attend (lines 107-113.3).
Mugasha then sends a hailstorm, and Wamara agrees, handing over his daughter to the wedding party (lines 114-114.8).
The party meets Mugasha at the shore and hands Wamara's daughter over; Mugasha worries about Kagoro (lines 115-121).

Mugasha returns to the lake with Wamara's daughter, while at Wamara's palace, Kagoro returns, inquires what happened, equips himself with bow and arrows, and goes to find the daughter (lines 122-125.3).
At the lake Kagoro calls to Mugasha, who tries to hide himself. Kagoro strikes Mugasha in the leg with an arrow (lines 126-132.2).
Mugasha cuts off his leg and decides to return Wamara's daughter (lines 133-134.8).

Mugasha orders Thunderstorm to split a boulder and trap Kagoro with only his head above. He beats Kagoro until he loses the power of rational speech. Mugasha concludes with a proverb on the importance of equal exchange (lines 135-147.3).
